title: Reading Practice (Generated)
description: LLM-generated reading comprehension drills, one lesson per VOA Learning English passage.

content.introduction: Read "Be Careful What You 'Bank On'" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: Banks help us manage our money. When we make deposits, we put money in; when we make withdrawals, we take money out.
prompt.question: What do banks help us to do?

prompt.passage: The first [idiom] is simply to bank on something. If you bank on something, you trust it will happen.
prompt.question: What does the phrase "bank on" mean?
options: To deposit money in a bank
To withdraw money from a bank
To borrow money from a bank

prompt.passage: When we have money in the bank, we count on it to be there when we need it. Banks offer guarantees that they will protect our money.
prompt.question: What does "money in the bank" suggest?

prompt.passage: If you laugh all the way to the bank, you make a lot of money with very little effort.
prompt.question: What does "laugh all the way to the bank" mean?
options: You are laughing at someone’s idea
You make a lot of money easily

answer_key.answer: You make a lot of money easily

prompt.passage: When a woman started a organic homemade dog food business, her friends thought it was a silly idea. They even laughed at her idea.
prompt.question: Why did the woman’s friends think her dog food business was a silly idea?
options: Because they thought it was a waste of time

answer_key.answer: Because they thought it was a waste of time

content.introduction: Read "The Story of Jack Frost" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: Jack Frost represents the coming of dark, cold days. He is the personification of winter, a human form of winter weather. So, when we say, “Jack Frost is nipping at your nose,” we mean that you feel the cold, icy effects of winter on your face.
prompt.question: What does the phrase “Jack Frost is nipping at your nose” mean?
options: You are smiling at Jack Frost
You feel the cold, icy effects of winter on your face
You are hearing music about Jack Frost
You are looking at a picture of Jack Frost

answer_key.answer: You feel the cold, icy effects of winter on your face

prompt.passage: When a frost or deep freeze comes, you had better bundle up! You should wear enough clothing to stay warm.
prompt.question: What should you do when a frost comes?

prompt.passage: Jack Frost can also create beauty in nature. Some traditions claim that Jack Frost is responsible for leaving ice and snow on windows, trees, the ground…anywhere it will stick.
prompt.question: According to the passage, what does Jack Frost leave behind?

prompt.passage: That it is NOT a winter coat. I mean, it looks great, but it’s much too thin. You will catch a cold. I know. You can wear my new down coat. It keeps you warm up to minus 30 degrees Celsius!
prompt.question: What temperature can the down coat keep you warm up to?

prompt.passage: If you’re wearing that thin coat…you’ll catch your death!! The EMTs will find you frozen solid like a block of ice!!
prompt.question: What might happen if you wear a thin coat in the cold?

content.introduction: Read "Sometimes We 'Spread Ourself Too Thin'" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: People who are spread too thin, take on too many jobs, projects, or goals.
prompt.question: What does it mean to be "spread too thin"?

prompt.passage: If we work on many projects simultaneously, or at the same time, it is possible that none will get done well, if they get done at all.
prompt.question: What might happen if we multitask too much?

prompt.passage: Being spread too thin can also be described as biting off more than we can chew.
prompt.question: Which phrase is another way to say "being spread too thin"?
options: Biting off more than we can chew

answer_key.answer: Biting off more than we can chew

prompt.passage: A: So, how are your classes going? B: Not great. Many of my best students are failing my course.
prompt.question: How are the students doing in the class?

prompt.passage: B: I think you’re right.
prompt.question: Who agrees that the students are spread too thin?

title: Reading Practice 2 (Generated)
description: LLM-generated reading comprehension drills, second batch of VOA Learning English passages.

lessons.title: 'Don't Look a Gift Horse in the Mouth'

content.introduction: Read "'Don't Look a Gift Horse in the Mouth'" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: "This idiom is really old. Way back in 1546, a man named John Heywood supposedly used this phrase in some Middle English text."
prompt.question: Which person is credited with using the phrase in 1546?

grounded_on.title: 'Don't Look a Gift Horse in the Mouth'

prompt.passage: "In other words, don't look a gift horse in the mouth."
prompt.question: What does the idiom mean?
options: To check the age of a horse
To find fault with a gift or favor
To ask for a better gift
To give a horse as a gift

answer_key.answer: To find fault with a gift or favor

grounded_on.title: 'Don't Look a Gift Horse in the Mouth'

prompt.passage: "But I am guessing a young horse makes a better gift than an old one."
prompt.question: According to the passage, which kind of horse is a better gift?
options: A horse that hasn't been checked

grounded_on.title: 'Don't Look a Gift Horse in the Mouth'

prompt.passage: "If you get something as a gift that you really don't like, you can always pass it along to someone else who may actually like it. In other words, you can always re-gift it!"
prompt.question: What can you do if you don't like a gift?
options: Ask the giver to return it

grounded_on.title: 'Don't Look a Gift Horse in the Mouth'

prompt.passage: "However, checking the horse's mouth would be a sign of mistrust towards the gift giver. This would be bad manners."
prompt.question: Why is checking a horse's mouth considered bad?
options: It shows mistrust towards the giver
It makes the horse feel upset

answer_key.answer: It shows mistrust towards the giver

grounded_on.title: 'Don't Look a Gift Horse in the Mouth'

content.introduction: Read "Exploring the 'Butterfly Effect'" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: Butterflies are delicate insects. Their wings are thin and can be easily damaged.
prompt.question: According to the passage, how are butterflies described?

prompt.passage: When a butterfly flaps its wings on one side of the planet it can make a small change that, over time, could lead to a big event, like a storm, all the way on the other side of the planet.
prompt.question: What can happen when a butterfly flaps its wings on one side of the planet?
options: a small change that could lead to a big event

answer_key.answer: a small change that could lead to a big event

prompt.passage: Dominoes are small rectangular game pieces. One way to play with dominoes is to set them up in a line or pattern. Then you push the domino at the start of the line into the one next to it and so on and so on … until all the dominos fall down.
prompt.question: What happens when you push the first domino in a line?
options: It causes a chain reaction where all dominos fall down

answer_key.answer: It causes a chain reaction where all dominos fall down

prompt.passage: The butterfly effect and domino effect are chain reactions. One thing leads to another. We often say that something sets off a chain reaction.
prompt.question: What are the butterfly effect and domino effect?

prompt.passage: Anna Matteo wrote this lesson for VOA Learning English.
prompt.question: Who wrote the lesson?

content.introduction: Read "When in Rome …" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: When visiting a foreign country, it is smart to follow the local social rules and customs of the people who live there. This can make your stay easier and more enjoyable.
prompt.question: Why is it smart to follow local social rules?
options: It helps you learn a new language
It makes your stay easier and more enjoyable
It guarantees you will be invited to parties
It guarantees you will get a job

answer_key.answer: It makes your stay easier and more enjoyable

prompt.passage: This expression means that when we visit a place, we should follow the customs and norms of that place.
prompt.question: What does the expression 'When in Rome, do as the Romans do' mean?
options: You should adopt the customs of the place you visit

answer_key.answer: You should adopt the customs of the place you visit

prompt.passage: For example, let’s say you usually speak very loudly. But when visiting an art museum or library, you whisper in a low voice.
prompt.question: What is an example of following the rules of a place?
options: Using a phone in a museum

prompt.passage: English speakers often shorten the expression to 'when in Rome…'
prompt.question: How do English speakers often shorten the expression?

prompt.passage: Some word experts say it first appeared in English around 500 years ago.
prompt.question: According to word experts, when did the expression first appear in English?

title: Reading Practice 3 (Generated)
description: LLM-generated reading comprehension drills, third batch of VOA Learning English passages.

lessons.title: Don't Miss a Thing With 'Eagle Eyes'

content.introduction: Read "Don't Miss a Thing With 'Eagle Eyes'" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: In English, we use the term “bird’s eye view” to describe a view from above as if seen by a bird in flight.
prompt.question: What is a "bird's eye view"?
options: view from above as if seen by a bird in flight

answer_key.answer: view from above as if seen by a bird in flight

grounded_on.title: Don't Miss a Thing With 'Eagle Eyes'

prompt.passage: Some birds, called raptors, are known for their extremely sharp vision. Eagles, hawks, and owls can see their prey from very far away.
prompt.question: Which birds are called raptors?

grounded_on.title: Don't Miss a Thing With 'Eagle Eyes'

prompt.passage: Eagles and hawks also have a special bone structure around their eyes that protects them from bright sunlight.
prompt.question: What protects eagles and hawks from bright sunlight?
options: special bone structure around their eyes

answer_key.answer: special bone structure around their eyes

grounded_on.title: Don't Miss a Thing With 'Eagle Eyes'

prompt.passage: If you have an eagle eye, you have the ability to see or notice distant objects. Also, you observe everything, even very small details which others might miss.
prompt.question: According to the passage, what does it mean to have an "eagle eye"?
options: you can see distant objects and notice details

answer_key.answer: you can see distant objects and notice details

grounded_on.title: Don't Miss a Thing With 'Eagle Eyes'

prompt.passage: The earliest evidence for ‘eagle eye’ is from 1567, in the writing of Thomas Stapleton, a Roman Catholic theologian.
prompt.question: When did the earliest evidence for the phrase "eagle eye" appear?

grounded_on.title: Don't Miss a Thing With 'Eagle Eyes'

content.introduction: Read "What Is Your 'North Star'?" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: Experts at NASA, America’s space agency, say this about the North Star: “If you're in the Northern Hemisphere, it can help you orient yourself and find your way, as it's located in the direction of true north …”
prompt.question: Who says the statement about the North Star?

prompt.passage: If you're in the Northern Hemisphere, it can help you orient yourself and find your way, as it's located in the direction of true north (or geographic north, as opposed to magnetic north).
prompt.question: Which type of north does the North Star point to?

prompt.passage: For some people, their North Star may be a religion. For others, it might be a purpose, like teaching people to read. Or a North Star can be a mix of important things.
prompt.question: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a possible North Star?

prompt.passage: The Merriam Webster online dictionary explains that both meanings of “lodestar” date back to the 1300s. The English poet Geoffrey Chaucer used both meanings of the word in his poems. However, by the 1600s, the literal meaning fell out of common use. Today, we still use “lodestar” in a figurative way meaning something or someone who guides us.
prompt.question: According to Merriam Webster, what does the word "lodestar" mean?
options: Only a star used to find one's way
Only a person who serves as an inspiration

prompt.passage: Another example might be when a music teacher and musician moved to a new city. He did not know anyone. He felt lost and alone. So, music was his North Star. It kept him busy, provided him with employment, and later led to great friendships with other musicians.
prompt.question: What served as the music teacher’s North Star?

content.introduction: Read "How to 'Dish Up' Something Good" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: So, a signature dish is a dish we are known for making well.
prompt.question: What does a signature dish mean?
options: a dish that is a person's favorite dish
a dish we are known for making well
a dish that we serve at parties
a dish that we sign with our name

answer_key.answer: a dish we are known for making well

prompt.passage: To dish can mean to talk about private or personal information. It might be gossip.
prompt.question: What does the verb "to dish" mean?
options: to talk about private or personal information

answer_key.answer: to talk about private or personal information

prompt.passage: We can also dish out things, like jokes or even insults.
prompt.question: What can you dish out?

prompt.passage: We say, “They can dish it out, but they can’t take it.” They are able and willing to share harsh thoughts, criticisms, or insults about others, but they do not think they, themselves, deserve such treatment.
prompt.question: What does the saying mean?
options: They can give food but cannot eat it
They can share harsh thoughts, criticisms, or insults about others, but they do not think they deserve such treatment
They can dish out money but can't keep it
They can dish out jokes but cannot laugh

answer_key.answer: They can share harsh thoughts, criticisms, or insults about others, but they do not think they deserve such treatment

prompt.passage: Now, to dish up something means to put food on a dish for someone to eat.
prompt.question: What does "to dish up" mean?
options: to put food on a dish for someone to eat

answer_key.answer: to put food on a dish for someone to eat

title: Listening Practice (Generated)
description: LLM-generated listening comprehension drills, one lesson per VOA Learning English passage, with the matching audio file.

content.introduction: Listen to "From Monkeys to Potatoes" and answer the questions.

prompt.transcript: When kids act up, parents or teachers may tell them to stop monkeying around. To monkey around means to do things that are not useful or serious, or to simply waste time.
prompt.question: What does "to monkey around" mean?

prompt.transcript: But for some reason – a reason that word experts do not know – we use “dog” in a phrase that means to feel unwell. If you are as sick as a dog you are really, really sick and will most likely stay home from work or school.
prompt.question: What does the phrase "as sick as a dog" mean?

prompt.transcript: This idiom means to reveal a secret or tell facts that were previously unknown. If you let the cat out of the bag, you spoil a surprise.
prompt.question: What happens if you let the cat out of the bag?

prompt.transcript: If you wear many hats you do many different things, even if you only have one job. For example, if you work at a small company, the owners may expect you to answer the phones, manage some paperwork, speak to clients, and design the web site.
prompt.question: What does "wear many hats" mean?
options: to keep a hat on your head

prompt.transcript: Being a couch potato is not a good thing. This means you sit on the couch all day and do nothing but watch television or play video games.
prompt.question: What does "couch potato" mean?
options: someone who sits on a couch all day

answer_key.answer: someone who sits on a couch all day

lessons.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

content.introduction: Listen to "'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun" and answer the questions.

prompt.transcript: Springtime is a time of renewal and growth. Many plants end their long winter sleep and form new buds and shoots. Beautiful flowers will soon be showing their bright colors. And the grass will grow thicker and greener again.
prompt.question: What happens to the grass in spring?

grounded_on.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

prompt.transcript: That idea is what gives us the expression “like watching grass grow.” We use this expression to describe an experience that is uninteresting. Another good word to describe it is tedious.
prompt.question: Which word is another way to describe the expression?

grounded_on.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

prompt.transcript: After the politician won his campaign, he gave a two-hour long acceptance speech. Two hours! It was as much fun as watching grass grow.
prompt.question: How long was the politician's speech?

grounded_on.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

prompt.transcript: For people who are always active, always moving, always on-the-go, we can say they don't let grass grow under their feet. We can also say beneath their feet.
prompt.question: What expression is used to describe active people?
options: let grass grow under their feet
don't let grass grow under their feet
let grass grow beneath their feet

answer_key.answer: don't let grass grow under their feet

grounded_on.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

prompt.transcript: Don’t let the grass grow beneath your feet. Get back to work.
prompt.question: What is the command given in the excerpt?

grounded_on.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

content.introduction: Listen to "'Kicking Off' a New Year" and answer the questions.

prompt.transcript: "What people around the world call “football,” Americans call soccer.",
prompt.question: "What do Americans call the sport that people around the world call football?"

prompt.transcript: "In addition to that, the game starts with a kickoff and, after scoring points, a team will kick off to the other side, beginning their turn on offense.",
prompt.question: "What does a kickoff do in American football?"

prompt.transcript: "When we punt the ball, you put it in play for the other team. The same can be said for punting in life. When we punt something, we give our task, job, or responsibility to someone else.",
prompt.question: "What does it mean to punt something in life?"
options: We give the task to someone else

answer_key.answer: We give the task to someone else

prompt.transcript: "If he succeeds, his team gets three points. ... In this case, your boss has moved the goalpost.",
prompt.question: "What did the boss do when the target changed from 10,000 to 20,000 followers?"

prompt.transcript: "Kicking a field goal can be difficult if the kicker is far from the goalposts or if the weather in an outdoor stadium is windy, rainy or snowy.",
prompt.question: "Why can kicking a field goal be difficult?"
options: Because the stadium is too small
Because the ball is too heavy
Because of windy, rainy or snowy weather

answer_key.answer: Because of windy, rainy or snowy weather

title: Listening Practice 2 (Generated)
description: LLM-generated listening comprehension drills, second batch of VOA Learning English passages, with matching audio files.

lessons.title: Knee Jerk and Gut Reactions: When We Are Not in Control

content.introduction: Listen to "Knee Jerk and Gut Reactions: When We Are Not in Control" and answer the questions.

prompt.transcript: We can call these decisions, actions or reactions knee-jerk.
prompt.question: What does the term "knee-jerk" refer to?

grounded_on.title: Knee Jerk and Gut Reactions: When We Are Not in Control

prompt.transcript: Meriam Webster defines the noun "knee jerk" as “an involuntary forward kick produced by a light blow on the tendon below the kneecap.”
prompt.question: According to Merriam-Webster, what is a knee jerk?
options: a light blow on the kneecap
an involuntary forward kick produced by a light blow on the tendon below the kneecap

answer_key.answer: an involuntary forward kick produced by a light blow on the tendon below the kneecap

grounded_on.title: Knee Jerk and Gut Reactions: When We Are Not in Control

prompt.transcript: A gut reaction also happens without thinking. But it is a bit different than a knee-jerk response. A gut reaction is a feeling. When we have a gut reaction, our instincts are telling us that something may not be right. Gut reactions are instinctual warnings.
prompt.question: How is a gut reaction different from a knee-jerk reaction?
options: Gut reaction is a feeling, knee-jerk is a thought
Gut reaction is a feeling, knee-jerk is an involuntary action
Gut reaction is a decision, knee-jerk is a feeling
Gut reaction is a decision, knee-jerk is a thought

answer_key.answer: Gut reaction is a feeling, knee-jerk is an involuntary action

grounded_on.title: Knee Jerk and Gut Reactions: When We Are Not in Control

prompt.transcript: One day, I was hiking on a mountain in California... I had a strange feeling... my gut instinct was to turn around and head back down the mountain... I later found out that a dangerous bear had been sighted...
prompt.question: In the hiking story, what did the narrator do when she had a gut instinct?
options: Turned around and went back down

answer_key.answer: Turned around and went back down

grounded_on.title: Knee Jerk and Gut Reactions: When We Are Not in Control

prompt.transcript: We often use knee-jerk in a criticizing or negative way.
prompt.question: Which sentence best reflects that knee-jerk reactions are used often in a negative way?
options: Knee-jerk reactions seem to happen automatically
We often use knee-jerk in a criticizing or negative way
Knee jerk also describes predictable behavior
Knee-jerk reactions or statements also seem like an involuntary reflex

answer_key.answer: We often use knee-jerk in a criticizing or negative way

grounded_on.title: Knee Jerk and Gut Reactions: When We Are Not in Control

content.introduction: Listen to "Partners in Crime" and answer the questions.

prompt.transcript: Partners in crime is an expression that describes two or more people who are very close to each other.
prompt.question: What does the expression 'partners in crime' describe?

prompt.transcript: Someone who is your partner in crime can also be called your sidekick. A sidekick is someone who is usually by your side.
prompt.question: What is a sidekick?
options: someone who is by your side

answer_key.answer: someone who is by your side

prompt.transcript: If your sidekick is helping you in a social situation, like meeting members of the opposite sex, then we can call them a wingman or wingwoman. In airplane talk, a wingman is a pilot who flies his aircraft behind the leading pilot.
prompt.question: In a social situation, what does 'wingman' mean?
options: a person who helps you meet people
a pilot who flies behind you

answer_key.answer: a person who helps you meet people

prompt.transcript: Although, you may want to be careful when using the word “accomplice.” When talking about a real crime, an accomplice is a person who helps others do something illegal.
prompt.question: When should you be careful using the word 'accomplice'?
options: When talking about a real crime

answer_key.answer: When talking about a real crime

prompt.transcript: Originally the expression may have described actual criminals. But today, it does not.
prompt.question: What did the expression 'partners in crime' originally describe?

content.introduction: Listen to "Reaching the 'Tipping Point'" and answer the questions.

prompt.transcript: The online dictionary Merriam-Webster defines tipping point as 'the critical point in a situation, process, or system beyond which a significant and often unstoppable effect or change takes place.'
prompt.question: According to Merriam-Webster, what happens after the tipping point?
options: A significant and often unstoppable effect or change takes place.

answer_key.answer: A significant and often unstoppable effect or change takes place.

prompt.transcript: Malcolm Gladwell wrote about the subject in his book Tipping Point published in 2002.
prompt.question: When was Malcolm Gladwell's book "Tipping Point" published?

prompt.transcript: Efforts taken now will not prevent environmental disaster. It is too late to stop the damage. In other words, that ship has sailed.
prompt.question: What does the phrase "that ship has sailed" mean?
options: It is too late to stop the damage.

answer_key.answer: It is too late to stop the damage.

prompt.transcript: The online Cambridge Dictionary defines the tipping point as 'a time during an activity or process when an important decision has to be made or when a situation changes completely.'
prompt.question: According to Cambridge Dictionary, what happens at the tipping point?
options: An important decision has to be made or when a situation changes completely.

answer_key.answer: An important decision has to be made or when a situation changes completely.

prompt.transcript: He describes tipping point this way: 'The tipping point is that magic moment when an idea, trend, or social behavior crosses a threshold, tips, and spreads like wildfire.'
prompt.question: In Gladwell's description, what does the tipping point spread like?

title: Listening Practice 3 (Generated)
description: LLM-generated listening comprehension drills, third batch of VOA Learning English passages, with matching audio files.

content.introduction: Listen to "Many Handy 'Hand' Expressions" and answer the questions.

prompt.transcript: These hand expressions could be called handy … meaning they are useful.
prompt.question: What does "handy" mean in this context?

prompt.transcript: I built it with my own bare hands.
prompt.question: What does "bare hands" imply?

prompt.transcript: If you lead with a firm hand, you are strict.
prompt.question: If you lead with a firm hand, you are?

prompt.transcript: A heavy hand is often considered a bad thing.
prompt.question: A heavy hand is often considered what?

prompt.transcript: Clean hands = innocent. But in this matter, your hands are clean.
prompt.question: What does having clean hands mean?

lessons.title: One Person's Trash Is Another Person's Treasure

content.introduction: Listen to "One Person's Trash Is Another Person's Treasure" and answer the questions.

prompt.transcript: When we say one person's trash is another person's treasure, we mean that something that one person may consider worthless could be highly prized or valued by someone else.
prompt.question: What does the expression mean?
options: It refers to how people throw away valuable items.
Something worthless to one can be valuable to another.
It describes how trash can become treasure.
It means everyone has the same taste.

answer_key.answer: Something worthless to one can be valuable to another.

grounded_on.title: One Person's Trash Is Another Person's Treasure

prompt.transcript: Now, rubbish and garbage mean the same as trash. But, in this expression, we use trash because the "tr" in trash and treasure makes the expression sound more natural.
prompt.question: Why is the word "trash" used in the expression?
options: Because "rubbish" is not a real word.

grounded_on.title: One Person's Trash Is Another Person's Treasure

prompt.transcript: Another expression with a similar meaning is different strokes for different folks.
prompt.question: Which expression has a similar meaning to the first one?

grounded_on.title: One Person's Trash Is Another Person's Treasure

prompt.transcript: It takes all kinds to make the world go round.
prompt.question: What does this expression mean?
options: The world needs many different types of people.
The world will only work if all kinds of things happen.
All kinds of people should go around.

answer_key.answer: The world needs many different types of people.

grounded_on.title: One Person's Trash Is Another Person's Treasure

prompt.transcript: To each his (her) own. Whatever floats your boat.
prompt.question: Which of the following expressions was NOT mentioned in the transcript?
options: All of the above were mentioned.

answer_key.answer: All of the above were mentioned.

grounded_on.title: One Person's Trash Is Another Person's Treasure

lessons.title: What Does It Take to Be a 'Power Couple'?

content.introduction: Listen to "What Does It Take to Be a 'Power Couple'?" and answer the questions.

prompt.transcript: In American English, we use many expressions to describe when two people have a lot of similarities or are very close. If we say two people are joined at the hip, they are nearly always together.
prompt.question: What does the expression "joined at the hip" mean?

grounded_on.title: What Does It Take to Be a 'Power Couple'?

prompt.transcript: If we describe individuals as thick as thieves, it means they are very close. People considered two peas in a pod have many things in common.
prompt.question: Which expression means people have many things in common?

grounded_on.title: What Does It Take to Be a 'Power Couple'?

prompt.transcript: But today we are going to discuss a very special kind of relationship – one that involves two people who are romantically involved and very successful. People in such relationships can be described as power couples.
prompt.question: What is a "power couple"?
options: Two people who are close friends
Two people who are romantically involved and very successful
Two people who work in the same office

answer_key.answer: Two people who are romantically involved and very successful

grounded_on.title: What Does It Take to Be a 'Power Couple'?

prompt.transcript:
A: Hey, I’m not used to seeing you alone. Where’s your sidekick? 
B: Huh? Who are you talking about? 
A: Ben. You guys have been joined at the hip for weeks.
prompt.question: Who is A speaking to?

grounded_on.title: What Does It Take to Be a 'Power Couple'?

prompt.transcript: Even though power couples involve highly successful people, one person could describe their partner as their better half. This is a way to compliment their “other half” in the relationship.
prompt.question: What is one way to compliment a partner?

grounded_on.title: What Does It Take to Be a 'Power Couple'?

title: Writing Practice (Generated)
description: LLM-generated sentence-correction and fill-in-the-blank drills, each grounded on one B1 grammar point.

lessons.title: Grammar Practice: there+AUX+be, You are, MODAL/AUX: may

content.introduction: Practice the grammar points covered in this lesson.

prompt.sentence: There ______ a new café near the station.
prompt.instruction: Fill in the blank with the correct form of 'be' to complete the sentence.

prompt.sentence: There is many books on the shelf.
prompt.instruction: Find and correct the error.

answer_key.answer: There are many books on the shelf.

prompt.sentence: ______ a good student.
prompt.instruction: Fill in the blank with the correct form of the verb to complete the sentence.

prompt.sentence: You is a good student.
prompt.instruction: Find and correct the error.
Fill in the blank with the correct word.
prompt.sentence: You ______ leave early if you finish your homework.

lessons.title: Grammar Practice: MODAL/AUX: may, FUNCTIONAL QUESTION: May I ...?, COMPARISON OF EQUALITY: not as/so ... as

content.introduction: Practice the grammar points covered in this lesson.

prompt.instruction: Find and correct the error.
prompt.sentence: I may not to finish the assignment before the deadline.

answer_key.answer: I may not finish the assignment before the deadline.

prompt.sentence: ______ you open the window?

content.introduction: Read "Dialogue 2-2: At the Doctor’s Office" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage:
DOCTOR: What seems to be the problem?
CATHY: Well, I have a bad cough and a sore throat. I also have a headache.
DOCTOR: How long have you had these symptoms?
CATHY: About three days now. And I’m really tired, too.
DOCTOR: Hmm. It sounds like you’ve got the flu. Take aspirin every four hours and get plenty of rest. Make sure you drink lots of fluids. Call me if you’re still sick next week.
CATHY: OK, thanks.

title: Reading Practice — B2 (Generated)
description: LLM-generated reading-comprehension drills based on O. Henry short stories (B2 literary prose).

content.introduction: Read "The Cop and the Anthem" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: For years the hospitable Blackwell’s had been his winter quarters.
prompt.question: Which place does Soapy usually use for his winter stay?

prompt.passage: In Soapy’s opinion the Law was more benign than Philanthropy. There was an endless round of institutions, municipal and eleemosynary, on which he might set out and receive lodging and food accordant with the simple life. But to one of Soapy’s proud spirit the gifts of charity are encumbered. If not in coin you must pay in humiliation of spirit for every benefit received at the hands of philanthropy.
prompt.question: How does Soapy feel about charity?
options: He thinks it’s a temporary solution
He thinks it’s better than law

prompt.passage: But as Soapy set foot inside the restaurant door the head waiter’s eye fell upon his frayed trousers and decadent shoes. Strong and ready hands turned him about and conveyed him in silence and haste to the sidewalk and averted the ignoble fate of the menaced mallard.
prompt.question: Why was Soapy turned away from the restaurant?

prompt.passage: In a cigar store he saw a well-dressed man lighting a cigar at a swinging light. His silk umbrella he had set by the door on entering. Soapy stepped inside, secured the umbrella and sauntered off with it slowly.
prompt.question: What did Soapy do when he found the umbrella?

prompt.passage: Through one violet-stained window a soft light glowed, where, no doubt, the organist loitered over the keys, making sure of his mastery of the coming Sabbath anthem. For there drifted out to Soapy’s ears sweet music that caught and held him transfixed against the convolutions of the iron fence.
prompt.question: What caused Soapy to have a sudden change of heart?

content.introduction: Read "Man About Town" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: It took me two weeks to find out what women carry in dress suit cases. And then I began to ask why a mattress is made in two pieces. This serious query was at first received with suspicion because it sounded like a conundrum. I was at last assured that its double form of construction was designed to make lighter the burden of woman, who makes up beds.
prompt.question: According to the narrator, why are mattresses made in two pieces?
options: To make it easier to fold
To make lighter the burden of woman, who makes up beds

answer_key.answer: To make lighter the burden of woman, who makes up beds

prompt.passage: “Why,” said he, “a ‘Man About Town’ is something between a ‘rounder’ and a ‘clubman.’ He isn’t exactly—well, he fits in between Mrs. Fish’s receptions and private boxing bouts.
prompt.question: How does the reporter describe a ‘Man About Town’?
options: A man who lives in the suburbs

prompt.passage: People passed, but they held me not. Paphian eyes rayed upon me, and left me unscathed. Diners, heimgangers, shop-girls, confidence men, panhandlers, actors, highwaymen, millionaires and outlanders hurried, skipped, strolled, sneaked, swaggered and scurried by me; but I took no note of them.
prompt.question: What did the narrator do when people passed by?
options: He tried to talk to them
He took a note of them
He took no note of them

answer_key.answer: He took no note of them

prompt.passage: He is a man who had a hopeless case of the peculiar New York disease of wanting to see and know. At 6 o’clock each day life begins with him. He follows rigidly the conventions of dress and manners; but in the business of poking his nose into places where he does not belong he could give pointers to a civet cat or a jackdaw.
prompt.question: According to the critic, what characterizes a ‘Man About Town’?
options: He is a man who had a hopeless case of the peculiar New York disease of wanting to see and know

answer_key.answer: He is a man who had a hopeless case of the peculiar New York disease of wanting to see and know

prompt.passage: Its headlines began where I heard the buzzing leave off the night before. It closed with these lines: “—Bellevue Hospital, where it was said that his injuries were not serious. He appeared to be a typical Man About Town.”
prompt.question: What did the hospital nurse say about the man?
options: He appeared to be a typical Man About Town

answer_key.answer: He appeared to be a typical Man About Town

content.introduction: Read "The Gift of the Magi" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: "One dollar and eighty‑seven cents. That was all. And sixty cents of it was in pennies. Three times Della counted it…And the next day would be Christmas.",
prompt.question: How much money did Della have to buy a present for Jim?

prompt.passage: "Now there were two possessions of the James Dillingham Youngs in which they both took a mighty pride. One was Jim’s gold watch…The other was Della’s hair.",

prompt.passage: "Jim drew a package from his overcoat pocket and threw it upon the table. …I sold the watch to get the money to buy your combs.",

prompt.passage: "Where she stopped the sign read: ‘Mme. Sofronie. Hair Goods of All Kinds.’ … ‘Will you buy my hair?’ asked Della. ‘I buy hair,’ said Madame.",

prompt.passage: "The magi, as you know, were wise men—wonderfully wise men—who brought gifts to the Babe in the manger.…They invented the art of giving Christmas presents. Being wise, their gifts were no doubt wise ones…Of all who give gifts these two were the wisest.",

Money is more important than love

title: Writing Practice — A1 (Generated)
description: LLM-generated writing drills grounded on A1-level grammar points (simple present, articles, basic prepositions).

lessons.title: Grammar Practice: I am, I am not, Am I ...?

content.introduction: Practice the grammar points covered in this lesson.

prompt.sentence: ______ am ready for class.
prompt.instruction: Complete the sentence to practise: I am.

prompt.sentence: I are ready for class.
prompt.instruction: Find and correct the error.

prompt.sentence: I ______ not tired today.
prompt.instruction: Complete the sentence to practise: I am not.

prompt.sentence: I is not tired today.
prompt.instruction: Find and correct the error.

prompt.sentence: ______ I late for the meeting?
prompt.instruction: Complete the sentence to practise: Am I ...?.

lessons.title: Grammar Practice: Am I ...?, Am I not ...?, You are not

content.introduction: Practice the grammar points covered in this lesson.

prompt.sentence: Are I late for the meeting?
prompt.instruction: Find and correct the error.

answer_key.answer: Am I late for the meeting?

prompt.sentence: ______ I not on the list?
prompt.instruction: Complete the sentence to practise: Am I not ...?.

prompt.sentence: Are I not on the list?
prompt.instruction: Find and correct the error.

answer_key.answer: Am I not on the list?

prompt.sentence: You ______ not on the wrong train.
prompt.instruction: Complete the sentence to practise: You are not.

prompt.sentence: You is not on the wrong train.
prompt.instruction: Find and correct the error.

answer_key.answer: You are not on the wrong train.

lessons.title: Grammar Practice: Are you ...?, Aren't you ...?, he/she is

content.introduction: Practice the grammar points covered in this lesson.

prompt.sentence: ______ you ready to start?
prompt.instruction: Complete the sentence to practise: Are you ...?.

prompt.sentence: Is you ready to start?
prompt.instruction: Find and correct the error.

prompt.sentence: ______ you coming with us?
prompt.instruction: Complete the sentence to practise: Aren't you ...?.

prompt.sentence: Isn't you coming with us?

prompt.sentence: She ______ responsible for the report.
prompt.instruction: Complete the sentence to practise: he/she is.

answer_key.answer: She is responsible for the report.

title: Writing Practice — C1 (Generated)
description: LLM-generated writing drills grounded on C1-level grammar points (advanced clause types, nominalisations, subjunctive).

lessons.title: Grammar Practice: PRONOUNS: the other/others, did DO, PASSIVE: FUTURE

content.introduction: Practice the grammar points covered in this lesson.

prompt.sentence: One proposal was rejected; ______ was approved.
prompt.instruction: Complete the sentence to practise: PRONOUNS: the other/others.

prompt.sentence: One proposal was rejected; other was approved.
prompt.instruction: Find and correct the error.

answer_key.answer: One proposal was rejected; the other was approved.

prompt.sentence: Only after the meeting ______ the director approve the plan.
prompt.instruction: Complete the sentence to practise: did DO.

prompt.sentence: Only after the meeting the director approved the plan.
prompt.instruction: Find and correct the error.

answer_key.answer: Only after the meeting did the director approve the plan.

prompt.sentence: The report ______ reviewed tomorrow.
prompt.instruction: Complete the sentence to practise: PASSIVE: FUTURE.

lessons.title: Grammar Practice: PASSIVE: FUTURE, PASSIVE: FUTURE PROGRESSIVE, PASSIVE: FUTURE PERFECT

content.introduction: Practice the grammar points covered in this lesson.

prompt.sentence: The report will review tomorrow.
prompt.instruction: Find and correct the error.

answer_key.answer: The report will be reviewed tomorrow.

prompt.sentence: At noon, the results ______ checked by the audit team.
prompt.instruction: Complete the sentence to practise: PASSIVE: FUTURE PROGRESSIVE.

prompt.sentence: At noon, the results will checking by the audit team.
prompt.instruction: Find and correct the error.

answer_key.answer: At noon, the results will be being checked by the audit team.

prompt.sentence: By Friday, the contract ______ signed.
prompt.instruction: Complete the sentence to practise: PASSIVE: FUTURE PERFECT.

prompt.sentence: By Friday, the contract will have signed.
prompt.instruction: Find and correct the error.

answer_key.answer: By Friday, the contract will have been signed.

lessons.title: Grammar Practice: PASSIVE: AUX, TO-INFINITIVE: to have DONE, MODAL/AUX: dare (to)

content.introduction: Practice the grammar points covered in this lesson.

prompt.sentence: The files must ______ stored securely.
prompt.instruction: Complete the sentence to practise: PASSIVE: AUX.

prompt.sentence: The files must stored securely.
prompt.instruction: Find and correct the error.

answer_key.answer: The files must be stored securely.

prompt.sentence: She appears ______ misunderstood the instruction.
prompt.instruction: Complete the sentence to practise: TO-INFINITIVE: to have DONE.

prompt.sentence: She appears to misunderstood the instruction.
prompt.instruction: Find and correct the error.

answer_key.answer: She appears to have misunderstood the instruction.

prompt.sentence: Few employees ______ to challenge the old rule.
prompt.instruction: Complete the sentence to practise: MODAL/AUX: dare (to).

content.introduction: Practice the grammar points covered in this lesson.

prompt.sentence: Few employees dares to challenge the old rule.
prompt.instruction: Find and correct the error.

answer_key.answer: Few employees dare to challenge the old rule.
